meta.title: Dylan Patel on the Dwarkesh Podcast
meta.blurb: Practice questions for Dylan Patel, drafted by the agent flashcard pipeline and human-reviewed before promotion.

sections.title: 00:00:00 – Why an H100 is worth more today than 3 years ago

cards.question_md: Why might an H100 become more valuable even after newer GPUs like Blackwell or Rubin arrive?
cards.answer_md: Better models, cheaper serving, and larger demand can raise the value of each old GPU's output faster than hardware obsolescence lowers it.
cards.question_html: Why might an H100 become more valuable even after newer GPUs like Blackwell or Rubin arrive?
cards.answer_html: Better models, cheaper serving, and larger demand can raise the value of each old GPU's output faster than hardware obsolescence lowers it.

sections.title: 00:00:00 – Why an H100 is worth more today than 3 years ago continued

cards.question_md: How can higher fixed compute costs push demand toward better (holding compute constant) AI models?
cards.answer_md: It is an Alchian-Allen style effect. If two options both inherit the same fixed cost, the higher-quality option becomes cheaper in relative terms. A $2 model versus a $1 model is 2x as expensive; add a $1 compute cost to both, and it is $3 versus $2, only 1.5x. If users must pay a large compute bill anyway, paying a bit more for the best model can look more attractive.
cards.question_html: How can higher fixed compute costs push demand toward better (holding compute constant) AI models?
cards.answer_html: It is an Alchian-Allen style effect. If two options both inherit the same fixed cost, the higher-quality option becomes cheaper in relative terms. A \(2 model versus a \)1 model is 2x as expensive; add a \(1 compute cost to both, and it is \)3 versus $2, only 1.5x. If users must pay a large compute bill anyway, paying a bit more for the best model can look more attractive.

sections.title: 00:34:34 – ASML will be the #1 constraint for AI compute scaling by 2030

cards.question_md: How does Dylan translate EUV tool throughput into a rough ceiling on AI data-center gigawatts?
cards.answer_md: Start with the wafers needed for 1 GW of AI chips: roughly 55k 3 nm wafers, 6k 5 nm wafers, and 170k DRAM wafers. Add up the EUV exposures across those wafers: about 2 million EUV passes. An EUV tool runs about 75 wafers/hour at about 90% uptime, so one AI gigawatt consumes roughly 3.5 EUV tools' worth of annual capacity.
cards.question_html: How does Dylan translate EUV tool throughput into a rough ceiling on AI data-center gigawatts?
cards.answer_html: Start with the wafers needed for 1 GW of AI chips: roughly 55k 3 nm wafers, 6k 5 nm wafers, and 170k DRAM wafers. Add up the EUV exposures across those wafers: about 2 million EUV passes. An EUV tool runs about 75 wafers/hour at about 90% uptime, so one AI gigawatt consumes roughly 3.5 EUV tools' worth of annual capacity.

cards.question_md: Why can't ASML simply double EUV production if demand for AI chips explodes?
cards.answer_md: EUV scanners are constrained by specialized sub-supply chains, not just ASML's willingness to spend. The tool depends on pieces like the Cymer light source, reticle stage, wafer stage, and Zeiss optics.
cards.question_html: Why can't ASML simply double EUV production if demand for AI chips explodes?
cards.answer_html: EUV scanners are constrained by specialized sub-supply chains, not just ASML's willingness to spend. The tool depends on pieces like the Cymer light source, reticle stage, wafer stage, and Zeiss optics.

cards.question_md: Why can't AI accelerators simply replace HBM with commodity DRAM to get more memory bits per wafer?
cards.answer_md: Because many inference workloads are bandwidth-limited, not just capacity-limited. DDR may give more bits, but if weights and KV cache cannot move fast enough, the expensive compute sits idle. HBM is costly in wafer-area terms because it buys much higher bandwidth, which is often the scarce thing.
cards.question_html: Why can't AI accelerators simply replace HBM with commodity DRAM to get more memory bits per wafer?

sections.title: 01:55:03 – Space GPUs aren't happening this decade

cards.question_md: What is the crux of the argument against space GPUs as a near-term solution for AI scaling?
cards.answer_md: Space optimizes for cheap power, but near-term AI scaling is not mainly bottlenecked on power. It is bottlenecked on chips.
cards.question_html: What is the crux of the argument against space GPUs as a near-term solution for AI scaling?
cards.answer_html: Space optimizes for cheap power, but near-term AI scaling is not mainly bottlenecked on power. It is bottlenecked on chips.

sections.title: 01:55:03 – Space GPUs aren't happening this decade continued

cards.question_md: Why might a frontier lab prefer training a smaller model for RL even if a larger model is more capable per rollout?
cards.answer_md: The smaller model can finish RL and deployment sooner. Even if a larger model is more sample-efficient, its rollouts cost more and take longer, which may more than offset the benefits.
cards.question_html: Why might a frontier lab prefer training a smaller model for RL even if a larger model is more capable per rollout?
cards.answer_html: The smaller model can finish RL and deployment sooner. Even if a larger model is more sample-efficient, its rollouts cost more and take longer, which may more than offset the benefits.

cards.question_md: Why might future robots rely on centralized cloud intelligence instead of carrying all intelligence onboard?
cards.answer_md: Cloud models can be larger and more efficient because they can batch inference across many tasks. The robot mainly needs low-latency local control: interpolation, grasping, force feedback, and other fast sensorimotor actions. Longer-horizon planning and richer world knowledge can be computed in the data center and sent back to the robot.
